श्रीभगवानुवाच |
क्षिप्रं भवति धर्मात्मा शश्वच्छान्तिं निगच्छति |
कौन्तेय प्रतिजानीहि न मे भक्तः प्रणश्यति ॥३१॥
iast:
śrībhagavānuvāca |
kṣipraṃ bhavati dharmātmā śaśvacchāntiṃ nigacchati |
kaunteya pratijānīhi na me bhaktaḥ praṇaśyati ||31||
explanation: Quickly does that person become righteous and attain lasting peace. O son of Kunti, declare it boldly — My devotee never perishes.
synthesis: Quickly the person becomes righteous and attains lasting peace. 'Declare it boldly — My devotee never perishes.' This verse extends the promise with divine urgency and guarantee. Shankara sees swift purification through the power of knowledge. Ramanuja reads the divine guarantee as the Lord staking His own honor. The bhakti tradition finds infinite reassurance in 'My devotee never perishes.' Madhva teaches that divine grace accelerates purification — this is God's personal promise. Abhinavagupta sees recognition's natural speed: the fundamental shift is instantaneous. Vallabhacharya is overwhelmed by the personal guarantee delivered through Arjuna. Tilak emphasizes lasting peace as the fruit of genuine, total commitment. Vivekananda seizes on 'declare it boldly' as the call to share this liberating message: no one is lost, no one is beyond hope.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that the speed of transformation is due to the power of self-knowledge combined with devotion. Once the resolve is made, the accumulated karmic momentum of sin is swiftly destroyed, just as a massive blaze quickly consumes fuel. Eternal peace (śāśvat śānti) is the natural state of the Self, restored when the clouds of sin part.

Ramanuja highlights that the Lord asks Arjuna to make this declaration so that the world will trust it — a human voice confirming divine truth is more immediately relatable. The guarantee that the devotee never perishes is the Lord's most solemn promise, backed by His absolute power. Devotion activates the Lord's protective grace, which ensures the devotee's eternal welfare.

The bhakti tradition cherishes this verse above almost all others. The Lord gives Arjuna the honor of being His spokesperson: proclaim it, so the world knows — My devotee never perishes. This is not a conditional promise — it has no fine print, no exceptions. It is the Lord's word, and it cannot fail. Every devotee carries this guarantee in their heart as an unshakable foundation.

Madhva teaches that the sinful person who turns to God 'quickly becomes righteous' because Vishnu's grace accelerates the process of purification. The command to 'declare boldly' (pratijānīhi) is Krishna's personal guarantee — making this not a hopeful wish but a divine promise backed by God's own honor.

Abhinavagupta sees the 'quickly becoming righteous' as the natural speed of recognition: once consciousness turns toward itself, transformation is not gradual but immediate. The old patterns may take time to fully dissolve, but the fundamental shift — from self-forgetting to self-recognition — happens in an instant. This is the swiftness Krishna promises.

Vallabhacharya is overwhelmed by Krishna asking Arjuna to declare — 'My devotee never perishes.' The Lord makes this declaration not through an abstract scripture but through His dear friend, making it personal and intimate. In pushti marga, this is the ultimate assurance: once claimed by Krishna's love, the soul is eternally safe.

Tilak emphasizes 'lasting peace' (shashvatim shantim) as the fruit: the reformed sinner who dedicates all action to God attains not temporary calm but permanent equanimity. This demonstrates the transformative power of consistent, purposeful action — even a late start yields enduring results when the commitment is genuine and total.

Vivekananda seizes on 'declare it boldly' as a call to share this liberating message: no one is lost, no one is damned, no one is beyond hope. This verse is Vivekananda's favorite because it destroys both despair and spiritual elitism in a single stroke. The practical message: start now, wherever you are, and know that you will not fail.

applications.personal_growth: When you genuinely commit to transformation, the change happens faster than you imagine. The momentum of sincere resolve is extraordinary. And the promise stands: those who commit to the path of truth do not ultimately fail. Declare this truth to yourself boldly.
applications.career_business: When a professional makes a genuine turn toward integrity and excellence, the market responds surprisingly quickly. Reputations can be rebuilt faster than you think when backed by authentic transformation. Your past does not permanently define your professional future.
applications.health: The body responds to genuine commitment with remarkable speed. When you truly resolve to care for yourself, healing mechanisms activate swiftly. The body has been waiting for your decision. Make it boldly and trust the process.
applications.relationships: When you genuinely commit to being a better partner, friend, or family member, the relationship transforms faster than years of half-hearted effort could achieve. Sincere resolve is felt by others immediately and inspires their own transformation.

श्रीभगवानुवाच |
मां हि पार्थ व्यपाश्रित्य येऽपि स्युः पापयोनयः |
स्त्रियो वैश्यास्तथा शूद्रास्तेऽपि यान्ति परां गतिम् ॥३२॥
iast:
śrībhagavānuvāca |
māṃ hi pārtha vyapāśritya ye'pi syuḥ pāpayonayaḥ |
striyo vaiśyāstathā śūdrāste'pi yānti parāṃ gatim ||32||
explanation: For those who take refuge in Me, O Partha — even those born of sinful wombs, women, vaishyas, and shudras — they too attain the supreme goal.
synthesis: Those of any background — even those born in difficult circumstances — attain the supreme goal by taking refuge in Krishna. This verse demolishes all social barriers to spiritual attainment. Shankara reads it as establishing the universality of Brahman-realization. Ramanuja sees God's grace reaching every category of being. The bhakti tradition finds that love knows no caste or gender. Madhva teaches that divine access transcends all worldly distinctions. Abhinavagupta declares consciousness accessible to all without exception — social categories are constructs of the contracted mind. Vallabhacharya reveals that pushti knows no boundaries of caste, gender, or status. Tilak reads this as revolutionary: equal access to spiritual truth implies equal dignity for all. Vivekananda considers this one of the most socially revolutionary verses in all scripture, fueling his mission of universal upliftment.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that the Self is equally present in all beings regardless of birth, gender, or social position. The categories mentioned represent those whom the conventional Vedic system considered ineligible for certain rites. Krishna transcends this system by offering a direct path — devotion to the Supreme — that bypasses all social qualifications. Knowledge of Brahman recognizes no caste.

Ramanuja emphasizes that the Lord's grace transcends all social distinctions. Those whom society considers low-born are as dear to the Lord as the highest-born sage when they take refuge in Him. This verse demolishes the notion that spiritual attainment is the privilege of the few — it is the birthright of all who surrender to God.

The bhakti tradition has historically championed this verse as proof that devotion transcends all social barriers. Saints from every background — from untouchable communities, from all genders, from all walks of life — have attained the supreme goal through pure devotion. The Lord sees no caste, no gender, no status — only the sincerity of the heart that turns toward Him.

Madhva teaches that this verse demonstrates Vishnu's grace reaching every category of being — the socially marginalized, women, merchants, and laborers all attain the Supreme through devotion. The mention of these categories does not validate social hierarchy but shows that divine access transcends all worldly distinctions.

Abhinavagupta reads this as consciousness declaring itself accessible to all without exception. Social categories — high or low birth, gender, occupation — are constructs of the contracted mind. In recognition, there is no caste, no gender, no hierarchy — only awareness meeting itself in every form. This verse abolishes spiritual elitism at its root.

Vallabhacharya teaches that Krishna offering the supreme goal to all social categories reveals the boundless nature of pushti — divine grace knows no boundaries of caste, gender, or status. In shuddhadvaita, every soul is an eternal part of Krishna. Social categories may structure worldly life, but they have no power in the realm of divine love.

Tilak reads this as revolutionary: if the supreme goal is attainable by all — regardless of social position — then the basis for spiritual discrimination collapses. The karma yogi extends this principle to active social life: equal access to spiritual truth implies equal dignity and equal opportunity for all members of society.

Vivekananda considers this one of the most socially revolutionary verses in all scripture. If God is accessible to the outcast, to women, to those of every class, then all social barriers to spiritual attainment are human inventions, not divine laws. This verse fuels Vivekananda's mission: uplift every person, for every soul is potentially divine.

applications.personal_growth: No disadvantage of birth, background, or past identity disqualifies you from the highest possible achievement. Whatever labels society or your own mind has placed on you, they do not limit your spiritual potential. Your access to truth depends solely on your willingness to seek it.
applications.career_business: Background does not determine professional destiny. People from every origin — regardless of education, socioeconomic status, or social connections — can achieve the highest levels of excellence when they fully commit. Do not let your starting point define your ceiling.
applications.health: Genetic predisposition, childhood circumstances, past trauma — none of these permanently define your health potential. When you fully commit to your wellbeing, you access healing resources that transcend your starting conditions.
applications.relationships: Your family of origin, your past relational failures, your social background — none of these disqualify you from experiencing the deepest possible love. The capacity for profound connection is inherent in every human being, regardless of background.

sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
किं पुनर्ब्राह्मणाः पुण्या भक्ता राजर्षयस्तथा |
अनित्यमसुखं लोकमिमं प्राप्य भजस्व माम् ॥३३॥
iast:
śrībhagavānuvāca |
kiṃ punarbrāhmaṇāḥ puṇyā bhaktā rājarṣayastathā |
anityamasukhaṃ lokamimam prāpya bhajasva mām ||33||
explanation: How much more then for righteous brahmanas and devoted royal sages! Having come to this impermanent and joyless world, engage in My worship.
synthesis: How much more so for the righteous and devoted! Having come to this impermanent and joyless world, engage in worship of Krishna. This verse combines spiritual urgency with universal invitation. Shankara reads the urgency as motivation for Self-knowledge. Ramanuja calls all — privileged and disadvantaged alike — to devotion. The bhakti tradition finds that life's brevity makes devotion urgent. Madhva addresses the instruction to everyone in this impermanent world — urgency is universal. Abhinavagupta sees consciousness calling itself to wake up within the dream. Vallabhacharya teaches that impermanence motivates urgency: love Krishna now, for life is brief. Tilak reads the urgency as a call to intensified purposeful action. Vivekananda draws the practical conclusion: wasting time on trivial pursuits is the greatest folly — use every moment for the highest purpose.

commentaries.text: Shankara emphasizes the description of the world as impermanent and without lasting happiness. This is not pessimism but discrimination (viveka) — recognizing the nature of the phenomenal world drives the aspirant toward the permanent. The command to worship is an invitation to seek Brahman, the only source of lasting peace.

Ramanuja reads this as the Lord's urgent call: you have found yourself in a fleeting, unsatisfying world — do not waste this human birth chasing its shadows. Whether you are born into privilege or hardship, the imperative is the same: worship the Lord and attain the eternal. The urgency in 'bhajasva mām' reflects the Lord's eagerness for every soul's liberation.

The bhakti tradition hears this as a loving plea from the Lord: you are in a world that cannot ultimately satisfy you — come to Me. The directness of 'bhajasva mām' (worship Me) cuts through all philosophical complexity. It is the simplest possible instruction: turn your heart to God, here, now, whatever your circumstance.

Madhva explains that if even the socially marginalized can attain God, how much more so the learned brahmanas and devoted royal sages who have every advantage. The instruction 'engage in My worship' is addressed to everyone in this 'impermanent and joyless world' — urgency is universal, not class-specific.

Abhinavagupta sees the urgency — 'having come to this impermanent world, worship Me' — as consciousness calling itself to wake up within the dream. The comparison between higher and lower social groups is dissolved by the shared condition: all are in an impermanent world. The practice of recognition is equally urgent for the scholar and the outcast.

Vallabhacharya teaches that the description of the world as 'impermanent and joyless' (anityam asukham) does not condemn creation but motivates the soul to seek its eternal source. In pushti marga, the world's impermanence is not a cause for despair but for urgency: love Krishna now, for life is brief and His love is eternal.

Tilak reads the urgency as a call to action: having arrived in this impermanent world, do not waste time in complacency. Whether born privileged or disadvantaged, the duty is the same — engage with the world through dharmic action and devotion. The karma yogi responds to impermanence not with withdrawal but with intensified purposeful effort.

Vivekananda draws the practical conclusion: if this world is impermanent and unsatisfying, then wasting time on trivial pursuits is the greatest folly. Use every moment for the highest purpose — spiritual realization and service to humanity. The verse's urgency is not pessimism but the supreme motivation to make this brief life count.

applications.personal_growth: Whatever your current station — privileged or challenged — this world is temporary and cannot provide ultimate fulfillment. This is not cause for despair but for clarity: invest your deepest energy in what endures, not in what passes away.
applications.career_business: Professional success in a temporary world is itself temporary. This does not mean abandon your career but recognize its limits. The wisest professionals build something meaningful beyond mere material success — legacy, service, contribution — that connects to the permanent.
applications.health: The body is inherently impermanent and is a source of both pleasure and suffering. Caring for it is wise, but placing ultimate hope in physical health alone is misguided. Use your health as a vehicle for something greater than mere physical existence.
applications.relationships: Every human relationship exists within the frame of impermanence — people change, depart, and pass away. This is not cause for nihilism but for urgency: love deeply now, because the opportunity is finite. Do not postpone what matters.

sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
मन्मना भव मद्भक्तो मद्याजी मां नमस्कुरु |
मामेवैष्यसि युक्त्वैवमात्मानं मत्परायणः ॥३४॥
iast:
śrībhagavānuvāca |
manmanā bhava madbhakto madyājī māṃ namaskuru |
māmevaiṣyasi yuktvaivamaātmānaṃ matparāyaṇaḥ ||34||
explanation: Fix your mind on Me, be devoted to Me, worship Me, and bow down to Me. Thus uniting yourself with Me and making Me your supreme goal, you shall certainly come to Me.
synthesis: Fix your mind on Me, be devoted to Me, worship Me, bow down to Me — thus making Me your supreme goal, you shall come to Me. This chapter's closing verse distills all teaching into four direct instructions. Shankara sees these as the complete program of Self-realization. Ramanuja reads them as the fourfold path of total surrender. The bhakti tradition hears Krishna's most intimate invitation. Madhva explains that each instruction addresses a different faculty — mind, heart, body, and ego — constituting total surrender. Abhinavagupta sees all movements converging on the single point of awareness — the recognition of what is already the case. Vallabhacharya receives this not as a command but as the beloved calling the lover home. Tilak reads the fourfold instruction as encompassing all aspects of active life: focus, love, duty, and humility. Vivekananda sees the simplest and most powerful teaching: dedicate yourself completely to the highest, regardless of birth or circumstance.

commentaries.text: Shankara interprets this as the culmination of both jñāna and bhakti: fixing the mind on Brahman is meditation, being devoted is maintaining constant awareness, worship is every action performed in this spirit, and bowing is the final dissolution of ego into the infinite. 'Coming to Me' is the realization that you were never separate from Brahman — it is not a journey to a place but an awakening to what is.

Ramanuja considers this the supreme instruction: engage every faculty — mind, heart, action, and will — in the Lord's service. The fourfold path ensures no part of the human being is left outside the sphere of devotion. The guarantee 'māmevaiṣyasi' is the Lord's personal promise, sealed by His divine power and unwavering love for the devotee.

The bhakti tradition regards this as the quintessential verse of devotion. It is Krishna's most direct and personal invitation: think of Me, love Me, serve Me, surrender to Me — and I promise you will reach Me. There are no prerequisites, no qualifications, no barriers. Just orient yourself completely toward the Lord, and the Lord takes care of everything else.

Madhva explains the four instructions — fix your mind, be devoted, worship, bow down — as the complete program of spiritual practice. Each addresses a different faculty: mind (thinking), heart (loving), body (ritual), and ego (humility). Together they constitute total surrender to Vishnu, which alone guarantees the soul's liberation.

Abhinavagupta sees 'making Me your supreme goal' as the final instruction of recognition: let all movements of mind, heart, and body converge on the single point of awareness. 'You shall come to Me' is not a future event but the recognition of what is already the case — consciousness returning to its own nature by ceasing to look elsewhere.

Vallabhacharya reads this concluding verse as Krishna's most intimate invitation: fix your mind on Me, love Me, worship Me, bow to Me. In pushti marga, each instruction is received not as a command but as the beloved calling the lover home. The promise 'you shall come to Me' is Krishna's personal guarantee of eternal union.

Tilak reads the fourfold instruction as encompassing all aspects of the active life: mental focus on God during all work, devotion that infuses every action with love, worship through one's daily duties, and humility before the divine purpose that exceeds personal understanding. This is the complete karma yoga program for daily living.

Vivekananda sees the chapter's conclusion as the simplest and most powerful teaching: dedicate yourself completely — mind, heart, body, and soul — to the highest reality. The promise 'you shall come to Me' is not conditional on birth, caste, or circumstance but on sincerity of effort. This is the universal teaching that makes the Gita a scripture for all humanity.

applications.personal_growth: The most powerful growth strategy is total alignment: direct your thoughts, feelings, actions, and ultimate allegiance toward your highest vision. Partial commitment produces partial results. When every dimension of your being points in the same direction, transformation is inevitable.
applications.career_business: Professional mastery requires total alignment: your mind thinks about your craft, your heart loves it, your actions serve it, and your deepest aspiration is oriented toward it. When thought, feeling, action, and purpose all converge, extraordinary achievement is guaranteed.
applications.health: Total health alignment means your mind thinks health, your heart loves wellness, your actions serve your body, and your deepest intention bows to life itself. When all four dimensions converge — mental, emotional, behavioral, and spiritual — healing is not a possibility but a certainty.
applications.relationships: The deepest love requires fourfold alignment: think of your beloved (mental presence), be devoted (emotional commitment), serve them (active care), and honor them (humble surrender of ego). When all four are present simultaneously, the relationship reaches its divine potential.
